chemicals.concentration_notes: These toxins are found in all parts of the plant, though the leaves are considered to be more toxic than the berries14. While severe mistletoe toxicity is uncommon and usually requires a cat to eat a large amount of the plant, small ingestions are still potent enough to cause mild to severe stomach upset
chemicals.description: in cats: These toxic proteins act as severe gastrointestinal irritants and cardiovascular depressants1.... When ingested, they can cause profuse vomiting and diarrhea, dilated pupils, and rapid, labored breathing2. Systemically, they affect the heart, causing a slowed heart rate and low blood pressure, which can ultimately lead to shock and death from cardiovascular collapse
chemicals.name: Lectins and viscotoxins (which are toxic proteins)

chemicals.concentration_notes: Phoratoxins are distributed throughout the plant1. Fortunately, a cat must typically consume a large amount of the plant for these chemicals to reach a severely toxic, life-threatening threshold
chemicals.description: in cats: Working alongside the lectins, phoratoxins specifically target the heart muscle and cardiovascular system13. They are responsible for inducing dangerous drops in blood pressure (hypotension) and abnormally slow heart rates (bradycardia), which can lead to profound weakness and odd behavior
chemicals.name: Phoratoxins

chemicals.concentration_notes: Amines are found in all parts of the mistletoe plant2. Like the other compounds, a large toxic dose is generally required to trigger fatal cardiovascular collapse
chemicals.description: in cats: Amines are present alongside the toxic proteins and contribute to the rapid onset of both gastrointestinal distress and cardiovascular shock2. They play a role in symptoms such as vomiting, diarrhea, dilated pupils, and labored breathing
chemicals.name: Amines

description: Mistletoe (Phoradendron spp. and Viscum album) is a common holiday decoration that is moderately toxic to cats. American Mistletoe (Phoradendron) contains phoratoxins — toxic lectins that affect the cardiovascular and gastrointestinal systems. The white berries are the most concentrated part and may appeal to cats. European Mistletoe (Viscum) additionally contains viscotoxins with stronger cardiac effects.
emergencyNote: Medically Induced Emesis and Activated Charcoal
name: Mistletoe
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: phoratoxins (toxic lectins) in all parts; viscotoxins in European Mistletoe; berries most concentrated
Mechanism: lectins inhibit protein synthesis in cells; cardiovascular effects include bradycardia and hypotension
Symptoms: vomiting, diarrhea, drooling, abdominal pain, weakness, bradycardia, low blood pressure; severe ingestion may cause collapse
All parts are toxic — fresh plant, dried decorations, and even fallen berries on the floor
Keep mistletoe completely out of reach during the holiday season; contact your vet if ingestion is suspected

symptoms.name: Gastrointestinal upset, including vomiting and diarrhea (which can be profuse)
symptoms.notes: If a cat only eats a small amount (like a few leaves or berries), the symptoms are typically limited to mild gastrointestinal irritation14. However, ingesting larger amounts leads to severe gastrointestinal distress56. Additionally, many store-bought mistletoe decorations use plastic berries; if a c

symptoms.name: Slowed heart rate (bradycardia), low blood pressure (hypotension), and potential cardiovascular collapse
symptoms.notes: These life-threatening cardiovascular effects are caused by the plant's lectins and phoratoxins3. Fortunately, severe heart toxicity is relatively uncommon and usually only occurs if the cat consumes a large amount of the plant34. If left untreated after a large ingestion, it can lead to shock and d
symptoms.onset: Within hours of ingesting a toxic dose

symptoms.name: Difficulty breathing or rapid, labored breathing
symptoms.notes: This symptom is typically seen in severe cases of toxicity alongside cardiovascular collapse and shock23. 4. Weakness, Odd Behavior, and Dilated Pupils
symptoms.onset: Within hours of ingesting a toxic dose

symptoms.name: Weakness, odd behavior, and dilated pupils
symptoms.notes: The profound weakness and lethargy a cat experiences are usually secondary effects caused by the significant drop in blood pressure and the slowed heart rate

treatments.description: If the mistletoe was ingested recently, the veterinarian will administer medication to induce vomiting and clear the highly toxic leaves or berries from the stomach. After vomiting, they will administer activated charcoal to bind to any remaining toxins in the digestive tract and prevent them from being absorbed into the bloodstream.
treatments.name: Medically Induced Emesis and Activated Charcoal
treatments.notes: This is the most urgent step to prevent the toxins from triggering severe cardiovascular and neurological symptoms. You should never attempt to induce vomiting at home without direct veterinary supervision. 2. Intravenous (IV) Fluid Therapy

treatments.description: The cat is placed on IV fluids to maintain hydration, support kidney function, and help flush the absorbed toxins out of the body.
treatments.name: IV Fluid Therapy and Blood Pressure Support
treatments.notes: Because mistletoe poisoning can cause profuse vomiting and diarrhea—as well as a dangerous drop in blood pressure (hypotension)—aggressive fluid therapy is critical to prevent severe dehydration and cardiovascular collapse. 3. Cardiovascular Monitoring and Pharmacological Support

treatments.description: The veterinary team will continuously monitor the cat's heart rhythm and blood pressure using an electrocardiogram (ECG). If the cat's heart rate drops dangerously low (bradycardia), the vet will administer specific cardiovascular drugs, such as atropine, to stabilize the heart rate.
treatments.name: ECG Monitoring and Heart Medications (e.g., Atropine)
treatments.notes: This targeted treatment is necessary because the phoratoxins in mistletoe directly depress the cardiovascular system. Cats that have ingested a large amount of the plant are at the highest risk for requiring emergency heart stabilization. 4. Symptomatic and Supportive Care

treatments.description: The veterinarian will administer anti-emetic medications to stop severe nausea and vomiting. If the cat is experiencing respiratory distress, supplemental oxygen will be provided.
treatments.name: Anti-emetics and Oxygen Therapy
treatments.notes: Even after the plant is removed from the stomach, the severe irritation to the mucosal lining of the gastrointestinal tract requires medical management to keep the cat comfortable and prevent further fluid loss.
